_meta.description: PawPal+ FAQ & Symptom Triage Knowledge Base — RAG retrieval corpus.
_meta.how_to_add: Append a new object to 'entries'. Required fields: id, question, answer, species, category, urgency, source, source_url, source_type. Optional: tags.

urgency_levels.emergency: Go to an emergency vet immediately.
urgency_levels.monitor: Watch at home; see a vet if it worsens or persists beyond 48 hours.

entries.question: My dog vomited once this morning. Should I be worried?
entries.answer: A single vomiting episode in an otherwise alert, playful dog is usually not an emergency. Withhold food for 2-4 hours to rest the stomach, then offer small amounts of water and a bland diet (plain boiled chicken and white rice). Monitor for repeated vomiting, blood in vomit, lethargy, bloated abdomen, or signs of pain — those warrant an immediate vet visit. Dogs that vomit more than twice in 24 hours, or any dog that appears distressed, should be seen by a vet promptly.

entries.question: When is dog vomiting an emergency?
entries.answer: Take your dog to an emergency vet immediately if vomiting is accompanied by: blood in the vomit (red or coffee-ground appearance), a distended or hard belly (possible bloat/GDV), repeated unproductive retching, suspected ingestion of a toxin or foreign object, collapse or extreme weakness, pale or white gums, or continuous vomiting for more than 12 hours. Bloat (Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus) is life-threatening and requires surgery within hours.

entries.question: My dog has diarrhea but seems fine otherwise. What should I do?
entries.answer: Mild diarrhea in a dog that is still alert and drinking water can often be managed at home for 24-48 hours. Fast your dog for 12-24 hours (adults only; never fast puppies), then transition to a bland diet of boiled chicken and white rice. Ensure fresh water is always available. If diarrhea is bloody, lasts more than 48 hours, is paired with vomiting, or the dog seems lethargic, contact your vet.

entries.question: My dog has bloody diarrhea. Is this serious?
entries.answer: Bloody diarrhea (hematochezia — bright red blood) or black tarry stools (melena — digested blood) warrant same-day or emergency veterinary care. Possible causes include hemorrhagic gastroenteritis (HGE), parvovirus, intestinal parasites, or colitis. Do not wait to see if it resolves. Bring a fecal sample if possible.

entries.question: My cat vomits occasionally. Is this normal?
entries.answer: Occasional hairballs once or twice a month can be normal. However, vomiting more than once a week, vomiting that contains blood, or vomiting with weight loss, increased thirst, or lethargy is NOT normal and needs veterinary attention. Chronic vomiting can signal inflammatory bowel disease, hyperthyroidism, or kidney disease.

entries.question: My cat hasn't eaten in 24 hours. Should I call the vet?
entries.answer: Yes — contact your vet if your cat has not eaten for 24-48 hours. Cats are at risk of hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver disease) in as little as 48-72 hours, especially overweight cats. Unlike dogs, cats should never be fasted as a GI treatment. A vet visit is needed to identify the underlying cause.

entries.question: My dog started limping suddenly. What could cause it and what should I do?
entries.answer: Sudden limping is often caused by a cut or thorn in the paw, a sprain, nail injury, insect sting, or joint injury. Check the paw for foreign objects, cuts, or swelling. If the dog is still bearing weight, rest for 24 hours. Do NOT give ibuprofen, acetaminophen, or naproxen — these are toxic to dogs. If limping is severe (non-weight-bearing), involves joint swelling, or does not improve within 24 hours, see a vet.

entries.question: My dog seems unusually tired and low energy. When is lethargy a red flag?
entries.answer: Mild lethargy after exercise or heat is normal. Seek emergency care for lethargy with: difficulty breathing, pale/blue gums, collapse, suspected poisoning, or a distended abdomen. Schedule a same-day vet visit for lethargy with vomiting/diarrhea over 24 hours, not eating, significant weight loss, coughing, or abnormal thirst.

entries.question: My male cat is straining to urinate or crying in the litter box. Is this an emergency?
entries.answer: YES — a male cat straining to urinate or producing only drops may have a urinary blockage (urethral obstruction). This is life-threatening; a blocked cat can die within 24-48 hours from kidney failure. Take your cat to an emergency vet immediately. Signs include frequent litter box trips with no urine output, vocalizing, licking genitals, lethargy, and vomiting.

entries.question: My dog has been coughing for two days. Should I be concerned?
entries.answer: A cough persisting more than 24 hours, or accompanied by nasal discharge, fever, lethargy, or appetite loss warrants a vet visit. Kennel cough (Bordetella) is a common, contagious cause especially after boarding or dog parks. A deep wet cough in an older dog can indicate heart disease or pneumonia and is urgent.

entries.question: My cat is sneezing a lot. What could be causing it?
entries.answer: Frequent sneezing with nasal discharge, eye discharge, or appetite loss suggests an upper respiratory infection (URI) — most commonly feline herpesvirus or calicivirus. These are highly contagious among cats. See your vet if sneezing has lasted more than 2-3 days, there is colored nasal discharge, or your cat seems unwell.

entries.question: My dog keeps shaking its head and scratching its ear. What's going on?
entries.answer: Head shaking and ear scratching signal ear discomfort. Common causes: bacterial or yeast ear infections, ear mites, foreign bodies, or allergies. Look for redness, dark discharge, or bad odor. Do not insert cotton swabs without vet guidance. Most ear infections require prescription drops; recurring infections may signal underlying allergies.

entries.question: My dog is scratching a lot and has red, irritated skin. What could it be?
entries.answer: Excessive itching with red skin has several common causes: environmental allergies, food allergies, fleas, contact dermatitis, or skin infections (hot spots, pyoderma). Check for flea dirt — small black specks near the tail base. Year-round scratching suggests food allergies; seasonal scratching points to environmental allergens. Your vet can perform skin tests and prescribe treatment.

entries.question: My pet ate something potentially toxic. What should I do right now?
entries.answer: Act immediately. Call the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center at (888) 426-4435 (24/7) or go to an emergency vet. Have ready: what was ingested, how much, and when. Common dangerous substances include xylitol, grapes and raisins, chocolate, onions, ibuprofen/acetaminophen, rodenticide, and many plants (lilies are fatal to cats). Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a vet.

entries.question: What common household plants are toxic to cats?
entries.answer: Most dangerous plants for cats: all true lilies (Easter, Tiger, Asiatic) — even small amounts cause acute kidney failure; Lily of the Valley — causes heart arrhythmias; Sago palm — toxic to liver; Tulip and Narcissus bulbs; Oleander; Azalea; and Pothos/Philodendron. If your cat chews on any lily, go to an emergency vet immediately — do not wait for symptoms.
entries.source: ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center — Toxic Plant List

entries.question: My dog seems overheated after being outside. What are signs of heatstroke?
entries.answer: Heatstroke is a life-threatening emergency. Signs: heavy panting, excessive drooling, red or pale gums, rapid heart rate, vomiting, stumbling, seizures, or collapse. Move your dog to a cool area, apply cool (not ice cold) water to paw pads and neck, and fan your dog. Never use ice water. Get to an emergency vet immediately even if symptoms seem to improve.

entries.question: How do I know if my dog has dental disease?
entries.answer: Signs of dental disease: bad breath, yellow or brown tartar buildup, red or swollen gums, pawing at the mouth, difficulty chewing, excessive drooling, and loose or missing teeth. By age 3, over 80% of dogs show signs of periodontal disease. Untreated dental disease is painful and bacteria can affect the heart, kidneys, and liver. Annual professional cleanings and daily brushing with dog-specific toothpaste are recommended.
entries.source: American Animal Hospital Association (AAHA) Dental Care Guidelines

entries.question: My senior cat is losing weight. What could be causing it?
entries.answer: Unexplained weight loss in a senior cat (over 10 years) always warrants a vet visit. Most common causes: hyperthyroidism (the #1 cause in older cats, very treatable), chronic kidney disease, diabetes, inflammatory bowel disease, cancer, and dental pain. Bloodwork and urinalysis are essential. A loss of 0.5 lb in a 10 lb cat equals a significant 5% body weight loss.

entries.question: My dog's eye is red and has discharge. What should I do?
entries.answer: Red eyes with discharge can indicate conjunctivitis, corneal ulcer, glaucoma, dry eye, or a foreign body. Clear discharge is often allergies; yellow or green discharge usually means bacterial infection. A red, painful eye with a cloudy cornea may be glaucoma — an emergency that can cause blindness within hours. Do not use human eye drops. See your vet same day if the eye is painful, swollen, or kept shut.

entries.question: How often should my pet see a vet for a wellness exam?
entries.answer: AAHA recommends annual wellness exams for healthy adult dogs and cats (1-7 years). Puppies and kittens under 1 year need visits every 3-4 weeks for vaccines. Senior pets (dogs over 7, cats over 10) benefit from biannual exams — age-related diseases progress quickly and early detection improves outcomes. Exams include weight check, dental assessment, heart/lung check, vaccine updates, and bloodwork for seniors.
entries.source: American Animal Hospital Association (AAHA) — Life Stage Guidelines
